input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided documents primarily focuses on **cyber-biophysical systems** (Document [7]) and **automotive systems** (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14], [17]), with additional references to **manufacturing systems** (Document [10]), **aerospace applications** (Document [16]), and **battery systems** (Document [17]). Key characteristics include:

1. **Cyber-Biophysical Systems (Document [7])**:
   - **Type**: Hybrid systems integrating computational, physical, and biological components (e.g., IoT-enabled medical devices, smart infrastructure, or industrial cyber-physical systems).
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Sensors, actuators, and embedded systems for real-time data acquisition (e.g., IoT nodes, edge devices).
     - **Cyber Layer**: Cloud/edge computing platforms for data processing, AI-driven analytics, and simulation.
     - **Biophysical Layer**: Biological subsystems (e.g., wearable health monitors, agricultural sensors, or bioprocessing units).
   - **Operational Domain**: Real-time monitoring, predictive maintenance, and adaptive control across **healthcare, agriculture, and industrial IoT**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Dynamic Interactions**: Bidirectional communication between physical and digital layers via **real-time data streams** (e.g., 5G/LoRaWAN connectivity).
     - **Multi-Domain Modeling**: Integration of **physical laws (e.g., fluid dynamics, thermodynamics)** with **biological models (e.g., physiological responses)**.
     - **Federated Architecture**: Decentralized data processing at the **edge** to reduce latency (Document [14]).
     - **Uncertainty Handling**: Incorporation of **probabilistic models** and **uncertainty quantification** for cyber-biophysical dynamics.

2. **Automotive Systems (Documents [8], [9], [12], [14])**:
   - **Type**: Connected and autonomous vehicles (CAVs), vehicle manufacturing lines, and **digital thread-enabled** automotive ecosystems.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Vehicles (e.g., EVs, self-driving cars) with **ADAS (Advanced Driver-Assistance Systems)**, sensors (LiDAR, radar, cameras), and telematics units.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) communication**, cloud-based **AI/ML models** for predictive analytics, and **digital twin frameworks** (e.g., ISO 23247-compliant).
     - **Operational Domain**: **Manufacturing (Document [10])**, **autonomous driving (Document [8])**, and **post-manufacturing lifecycle management**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Physical-Digital Convergence**: Seamless synchronization between **real-time vehicle telemetry** and **digital replicas** for **predictive maintenance** and **fault diagnosis** (Document [8]).
     - **Digital Thread**: End-to-end data flow from **design (CAD/CAM)** to **manufacturing (PLM)** to **operation (IoT)** (Document [10]).
     - **Edge Computing**: Localized processing of **high-frequency sensor data** (e.g., 100Hz+ from LiDAR) to reduce cloud dependency (Document [14]).
     - **Regulatory Compliance**: Alignment with **ISO 23247** for **manufacturing digital twins** and **ISO/IEC 30173** for **terminology standardization** (Documents [10], [11]).

3. **Aerospace Systems (Document [16])**:
   - **Type**: **On-orbit satellite systems** for collision avoidance and **space debris detection**.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Satellites with **GPS, radar, and optical sensors** for real-time orbital tracking.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **AI-driven collision prediction models** and **digital twin frameworks adapted from ISO 23247**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Real-Time Adaptation**: Dynamic updates to **orbital mechanics models** based on **real-time sensor data** (e.g., Doppler radar, star trackers).
     - **Standardization**: Adoption of **ISO 23247** for **manufacturing-inspired digital twin principles** applied to **aerospace asset management**.

4. **Battery Systems (Document [17])**:
   - **Type**: **Lithium-ion battery packs** in electric vehicles (EVs) or grid storage.
   - **Main Components**:
     - **Physical Layer**: Battery cells, BMS (Battery Management System), and thermal management units.
     - **Cyber Layer**: **Digital twin framework** integrating **electrochemical models**, **thermal simulations**, and **degradation prediction algorithms**.
   - **Key Characteristics**:
     - **Multi-Physics Simulation**: Coupling of **electrochemical (EIS), thermal (CFD), and mechanical (stress analysis)** models.
     - **ISO 23247 Compliance**: Use of **standardized digital twin principles** for **lifecycle monitoring and failure prediction**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** systems described in the documents provide **domain-specific services** categorized by **monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, and visualization**, targeting **engineers, operators, manufacturers, and AI/ML developers**. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring Services (Documents [7], [8], [10], [11], [12], [14])**:
   - **Real-Time Telemetry**: Continuous streaming of **sensor data** (e.g., vehicle telemetry, battery voltage, temperature) to the DT for **anomaly detection** (Document [8]).
   - **Health Monitoring**: **Condition-based monitoring** of **cyber-biophysical systems** (e.g., industrial machinery, satellites) using **vibration analysis, acoustic emission, and thermal imaging** (Document [7]).
   - **Digital Shadow**: A **real-time digital replica** of the physical system updated via **edge-to-cloud pipelines** (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**: **Maintenance engineers, fleet managers, and operations teams**.

2. **Optimization Services (Documents [7], [9], [14], [15])**:
   - **Resource Allocation**: Optimization of **energy consumption** in automotive DTs (e.g., **regenerative braking strategies**) and **manufacturing DTs** (e.g., **production line scheduling**) (Document [9]).
   - **AI-Driven Optimization**: Use of **reinforcement learning (RL)** and **genetic algorithms** to optimize **vehicle routing, battery charging cycles, and industrial workflows** (Document [15]).
   - **Target Users**: **Supply chain managers, automotive engineers, and energy grid operators**.

3. **Prediction Services (Documents [7], [8], [9], [11], [16])**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: **Machine learning models** (e.g., **LSTM, Random Forests**) trained on **historical and real-time data** to predict **equipment failures** (e.g., **bearing wear in automotive engines**, **satellite component degradation**) (Document [7], [16]).
   - **Lifespan Forecasting**: **Degradation modeling** for **battery systems** (Document [17]) and **aerospace components** using **physics-of-failure (PoF) approaches**.
   - **Traffic/Prediction**: **Autonomous vehicle path optimization** via **DT-based simulation of traffic scenarios** (Document [8]).
   - **Target Users**: **Predictive maintenance teams, R&D engineers, and asset managers**.

4. **Control Services (Documents [7], [8], [14])**:
   - **Closed-Loop Control**: **Real-time adjustment** of **actuators** (e.g., **throttle control in EVs**, **thermal management in batteries**) based on DT simulations (Document [8]).
   - **Adaptive Control**: **Edge-based control algorithms** for **low-latency applications** (e.g., **autonomous driving**, **industrial robotics**) (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**: **Control systems engineers, autonomous vehicle developers**.

5. **Visualization Services (Documents [10], [11], [12], [15])**:
   - **3D/4D Visualization**: **Interactive digital replicas** of **manufacturing plants, vehicles, and satellites** using **Unreal Engine, Blender, or ISO 23247-compliant visualization tools** (Document [10]).
   - **Augmented Reality (AR)**: **AR overlays** for **remote maintenance** and **training simulations** (Document [12]).
   - **Data Dashboards**: **Real-time KPI visualization** (e.g., **energy efficiency, defect rates**) via **Power BI, Tableau, or custom DT portals**.
   - **Target Users**: **Executives, technicians, and end-users**.

6. **Simulation Services (Documents [7], [15], [16])**:
   - **Multi-Physics Simulation**: **Coupled simulations** of **electromechanical, thermal, and fluid dynamics** for **automotive and aerospace DTs** (Document [15]).
   - **AI Simulation**: **Neural network-driven simulations** for **predictive modeling** (e.g., **battery degradation**, **collision avoidance**) (Document [15]).
   - **Target Users**: **Simulation engineers, R&D teams**.

7. **Standardization Services (Documents [10], [11], [12], [16])**:
   - **ISO 23247 Compliance**: **Framework alignment** for **manufacturing, automotive, and aerospace DTs** (Documents [10], [16]).
   - **Terminology Standardization**: **ISO/IEC 30173** for **consistent DT terminology** across industries (Document [11]).
   - **Target Users**: **Standardization bodies, DT framework developers**.

8. **Security and Privacy Services (Document [7])**:
   - **Data Encryption**: **End-to-end encryption** for **cyber-biophysical DTs** to protect **sensitive health/industrial data**.
   - **Access Control**: **Role-based access management (RBAC)** for **secure DT interactions**.
   - **Target Users**: **Cybersecurity teams, compliance officers**.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin (DT) systems in the provided documents include **software platforms, simulation engines, edge computing frameworks, databases, and specialized technologies**. Key components are:

1. **Software Platforms (Documents [7], [10], [11], [12], [14], [15], [16])**:
   - **Digital Twin Frameworks**:
     - **ISO 23247-Compliant Frameworks** (Documents [10], [14], [16]): Standardized architecture for **manufacturing, automotive, and aerospace DTs**, including **data integration, simulation, and visualization layers**.
     - **Edge Computing Frameworks** (Document [14]): **Kubernetes-based edge platforms** (e.g., **K3s, OpenYurt**) for **low-latency DT processing** in automotive and industrial IoT.
   - **Simulation Engines**:
     - **Multi-Physics Simulation Tools**:
       - **ANSYS, COMSOL** (Document [15]): For **electromechanical, thermal, and fluid dynamics simulations** in automotive and aerospace DTs.
       - **MATLAB/Simulink** (Document [7]): For **control system design and real-time DT simulations**.
     - **AI Simulation Tools**:
       - **TensorFlow, PyTorch** (Document [15]): For **neural network-based DT simulations** (e.g., **battery degradation prediction**).
       - **OpenVINO** (Document [14]): For **optimized AI inference at the edge**.
   - **Databases**:
     - **Time-Series Databases**: **InfluxDB, TimescaleDB** (Document [7]) for **real-time sensor data storage**.
     - **Graph Databases**: **Neo4j** (Document [12]) for **relationship modeling** in **systems of twinned systems**.
     - **NoSQL Databases**: **MongoDB, Cassandra** (Document [14]) for **scalable DT data storage**.

2. **Development Tools (Documents [7], [12], [15])**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) Tools**:
     - **SysML, UML** (Document [7]): For **DT system modeling and architecture design**.
     - **Modelica** (Document [15]): For **multi-domain simulation modeling**.
   - **Version Control**:
     - **Git, GitLab, Azure DevOps** (Document [12]): For **collaborative DT development**.
   - **APIs and Protocols**:
     - **RESTful APIs, gRPC** (Document [14]): For **inter-service communication** in DT systems.
     - **MQTT, AMQP** (Document [7]): For **real-time data streaming** from IoT devices.

3. **Edge Computing Technologies (Documents [7], [14], [16])**:
   - **Edge Devices**:
     - **Raspberry Pi, NVIDIA Jetson** (Document [14]): For **local DT processing** in automotive and industrial applications.
     - **FPGA/ASIC Accelerators** (Document [16]): For **real-time aerospace DT computations**.
   - **Edge Frameworks**:
     - **AWS IoT Greengrass, Azure IoT Edge** (Document [14]): For **decentralized DT analytics**.
     - **Kubernetes (K8s) on Edge** (Document [14]): For **scaling DT workloads** across edge nodes.

4. **Cloud Platforms (Documents [7], [14], [15])**:
   - **Cloud Providers**:
     - **AWS, Azure, Google Cloud** (Document [14]): For **scalable DT storage and AI/ML training**.
   - **Serverless Computing**:
     - **AWS Lambda, Azure Functions** (Document [15]): For **event-driven DT services**.

5. **Visualization Tools (Documents [10], [12], [15])**:
   - **3D/4D Visualization**:
     - **Unreal Engine, Blender, Unity** (Document [10]): For **immersive DT visualization**.
     - **ParaView, VisIt** (Document [15]): For **scientific DT visualization**.
   - **AR/VR Tools**:
     - **Microsoft HoloLens, Meta Quest** (Document [12]): For **AR-based DT interaction**.

6. **AI/ML Enablers (Documents [7], [15], [16])**:
   - **AI Libraries**:
     - **scikit-learn, XGBoost** (Document [7]): For **classical ML models** in DTs.
     - **TensorFlow Extended (TFX), PyTorch Lightning** (Document [15]): For **scalable AI/ML pipelines**.
   - **Explainable AI (XAI)**:
     - **SHAP, LIME** (Document [15]): For **interpretable DT predictions**.

7. **Standardization and Interoperability (Documents [10], [11], [12], [16])**:
   - **Standards**:
     - **ISO 23247** (Documents [10], [14], [16]): **Digital Twin framework** for manufacturing, automotive, and aerospace.
     - **ISO/IEC 30173** (Document [11]): **Terminology and concepts** for DTs.
     - **OPC UA** (Document [12]): For **industrial IoT interoperability**.
   - **Semantic Web Technologies**:
     - **RDF, SPARQL** (Document [12]): For **ontology-based DT knowledge representation**.

8. **Security and Privacy Tools (Document [7])**:
   - **Encryption**:
     - **AES-256, TLS 1.3** (Document [7]): For **secure DT data transmission**.
   - **Blockchain**:
     - **Hyperledger Fabric** (Document [7]): For **immutable DT audit logs**.
   - **Access Control**:
     - **OpenID Connect, OAuth 2.0** (Document [7]): For **secure DT user authentication**.

9. **Specialized Technologies (Documents [7], [16])**:
   - **Cyber-Biophysical Systems**:
     - **Wearable Sensors (e.g., ECG, EEG)** (Document [7]): For **health DTs**.
     - **Biometric Authentication** (Document [7]): For **secure DT access**.
   - **Aerospace DTs**:
     - **Orbital Mechanics Simulators** (Document [16]): For **collision avoidance DTs**.
     - **Space Debris Tracking Algorithms** (Document [16]): For **real-time DT updates**.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination, adhering to stricter vehicular application requirements despite hardware limitations. Temporal granularity is dictated by the ROS2-based architecture, where each Function Entity (FE) executes as an independent ROS2 package with nodes handling real-time data processing (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). Latency requirements are implicitly constrained by the ROS2 middleware’s deterministic communication, though explicit latency benchmarks for end-to-end services (e.g., sensor-to-actuator loops) are not documented. The system’s temporal alignment relies on shared time synchronization protocols mandated by ISO 23247, ensuring consistency across distributed entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, and User Entity).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based architecture** with three primary multiplicities: the *Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*, each deployed on distinct hardware components. This structure diverges from ISO 23247’s standard by consolidating resource-specific functionality (e.g., sensors, actuators, compute) into the *Physical Twin Entity*, enabling broader automotive use cases like *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*. The *User Entity* is further subdivided into two subcomponents (*3D Visualization* and *Control Panel*), while the *Data Storage* component lacks a direct ISO 23247 alignment. Coordination between entities is achieved via ROS2’s inter-node communication, with no explicit mention of hierarchical control or federated twin instances. Responsibilities are partitioned by functional domain: the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data/control mechanisms, while the *Physical Twin Entity* handles pre-processing and actuation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts a **component-based orchestration pattern** rooted in ISO 23247’s Reference Architecture (RA), where Function Entities (FEs) are mapped to ROS2 packages as modular, executable units. Each FE encapsulates a specific functionality (e.g., *User Interface FE* split into visualization/control panel) and communicates via ROS2 topics/services, enabling loose coupling and dynamic reconfiguration. Resource management is implicit, with entities deployed on separate hardware to optimize performance and isolation. System-wide coordination relies on ROS2’s middleware for real-time synchronization (e.g., 100 ms clock alignment) and ROS2’s Quality of Service (QoS) policies to prioritize critical data streams. The architecture deviates from ISO 23247 by expanding the *Physical Twin Entity* to include general automotive functionalities, potentially impacting ownership and engineering processes. No mention of cloud-edge orchestration or federated learning mechanisms is provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** reference architecture, which defines an **entity-based framework** where computational models and data components are structured into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**—physical entities (e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel, or processes) with observable attributes or operations. The system employs **three primary entities** for model and data management:
1. **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**: Responsible for **real-time data acquisition** from OMEs via standardized protocols (e.g., **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)**), transforming raw sensor data into structured formats for downstream processing.
2. **Digital Twin Entity**: Hosts **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** of OMEs, including **CAD-based representations**, **physics-based simulations**, and **rule-based behavioral models** to replicate physical and operational dynamics. Models are **entity-specific**, with **Functional Entities (FEs)** (e.g., data transformation modules, simulation kernels) orchestrating interactions between data sources and twin representations.
3. **User Entity**: Provides **human-machine interfaces** for monitoring (e.g., dashboards) and control, leveraging **model outputs** (e.g., predictive analytics, anomaly detection) to visualize or act upon OME states.

**Data Sources**: Primarily **sensor networks**, **IoT devices**, and **historical manufacturing databases**, with **temporal synchronization** ensured via timestamped data feeds. **Data Management Approaches** include **centralized repositories** for OME-specific datasets, **versioning mechanisms** for model updates, and **interoperability layers** (e.g., OPC UA) to facilitate cross-entity communication. **Model Relationships** are defined via **entity dependencies**: FEs in the Digital Twin Entity reference data streams from the Data Collection Entity and expose interfaces to the User Entity for decision-making. The standard does not specify **fidelity metrics** or **uncertainty quantification** but implies **closed-loop validation** through real-time comparisons between twin predictions and OME observations.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components:
The Digital Twin system’s sensing infrastructure is implicitly defined by the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** in the **ISO 23247** reference architecture, which mandates **real-time data acquisition from Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)**. While specific **sensor types**, **spatial distributions**, or **sampling frequencies** are not detailed in the provided documents, the architecture assumes:
- **Measurement Capabilities**: Sensors must capture **observable physical attributes** (e.g., temperature, pressure, vibration, position) and **operational states** (e.g., equipment status, process parameters) of OMEs.
- **Data Transmission**: Data is transmitted via **standardized protocols** (e.g., **OPC UA**), enabling **low-latency communication** between sensors and the Digital Twin Entity.
- **Accuracy/Precision**: Implicitly tied to **ISO 23247’s validation requirements**, though no explicit specifications (e.g., ±X% error margins) are provided.
- **Spatial Distribution**: Sensors are deployed **co-located with OMEs** to ensure **spatial alignment** between physical and digital representations, with no mention of **wireless vs. wired** deployment or **redundancy mechanisms**.

The architecture does not specify **sampling frequencies**, **data rates**, or **calibration procedures**, but the **real-time requirement** for the Data Collection Entity implies **high-frequency updates** (e.g., ≥1 Hz for dynamic OMEs like machinery). **Data Collection Mechanisms** rely on **entity-specific FEs** to aggregate, preprocess, and forward sensor data to the Digital Twin Entity for twin updates.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The **ISO 23247** standard outlines **fidelity and validity considerations** for Digital Twins through its **entity-based reference architecture**, though explicit **validation methods**, **uncertainty quantification**, or **error correction mechanisms** are not detailed. Key implicit and explicit requirements include:
1. **Model Fidelity Requirements**: The **Digital Twin Entity** must replicate OME behaviors with **sufficient accuracy** to support **predictive monitoring, control, and decision-making**, but **quantitative fidelity metrics** (e.g., RMSE, confidence intervals) are not specified. Fidelity is achieved via:
   - **Geometric/Behavioral Models**: Aligned with **physical OME specifications** (e.g., CAD models for equipment, process flow diagrams for manufacturing steps).
   - **Analytical Models**: Incorporating **physics-based simulations** (e.g., finite element analysis for stress testing) and **rule-based logic** for operational workflows.
2. **Validation and Verification (V&V) Methods**: The standard implies **closed-loop validation** where the Digital Twin Entity’s outputs are **compared against real-time OME observations** via the Data Collection Entity. However, no **formal V&V protocols** (e.g., ISO 15504, DO-178C) or **automated testing frameworks** are mentioned.
3. **Uncertainty Sources and Quantification**: Uncertainty arises from:
   - **Sensor Noise** (implied via data transmission via OPC UA, which lacks explicit error modeling).
   - **Model Simplifications** (e.g., approximations in behavioral FEs).
   - **Environmental Variability** (e.g., temperature fluctuations affecting OME performance).
   Quantification methods are **not specified**, but the architecture suggests **real-time error detection** via **discrepancy analysis** between twin predictions and observed OME states.
4. **Error Detection and Correction**: The **Data Collection Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity** must implement **feedback loops** to correct discrepancies, though no **specific algorithms** (e.g., Kalman filters, machine learning-based corrections) or **thresholds for intervention** are defined.
5. **Quality Assurance (QA) Processes**: The standard mandates **entity-specific QA** for each Functional Entity (FE), ensuring:
   - **Data Integrity**: Validated via **timestamp synchronization** and **source authentication** in OPC UA communications.
   - **Model Consistency**: Achieved through **periodic recalibration** of Digital Twin models against OME ground truth (e.g., manual inspections, calibration certifications).
   - **Interoperability**: Guaranteed by **standardized interfaces** (OPC UA) and **semantic consistency** in entity interactions.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **Physical-to-Virtual Interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves real-time data acquisition from physical sensors and their transformation into digital representations. **Data flows** originate from LiDAR, motor, steering servo, and vehicle state sensors, which feed raw **point cloud data** and **vehicle state information** into the **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)**. The **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** SW component processes raw LiDAR data (individual 2D/3D points) into structured object tracks and environmental representations, ensuring **data quality assurance** through clustering algorithms. **Preprocessing steps** include:
  - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** (converts raw LiDAR points into object-centric data).
  - **Data Pre-Processing FE** (standardizes sensor inputs like motor/steering actuation signals).
  - **Data Collection FE** (aggregates processed data for storage/reporting).
**Data integration mechanisms** rely on **ROS2** for intra-system communication and **UDP over Wi-Fi** for inter-entity transmission. **Transmission frequencies** are not explicitly stated, but real-time monitoring implies high-frequency updates (e.g., ≥10Hz for LiDAR/vehicle state). **Event triggers** include sensor activations (e.g., LiDAR scans) and system-initiated data collection for reporting. **Data quality** is implicitly ensured via structured preprocessing (e.g., clustering) and alignment with ISO 23247-compliant **Data Translation FE** for serialization and unit conversion.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **Virtual-to-Physical Interaction** in this DTS is facilitated by the **Controlling FE (ACC)**, which translates digital commands into physical actuation. **Control flows** originate from the **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** subsystem, where **command types** include:
  - **Motor Actuation** (speed adjustments via servo signals).
  - **Steering Actuation** (directional control via servo commands).
  - **Vehicle State Adjustments** (e.g., braking/acceleration overrides).
**Control message formats** are serialized and aligned via the **Data Translation FE** (ISO 23247-compliant), ensuring compatibility across ROS2 and UDP networks. **Validation mechanisms** are implied through the **Data Translation FE’s** unit conversions and message alignment, though explicit authentication is not documented. **Feedback systems** rely on **real-time vehicle state data** (e.g., speed, steering angle) fed back into the **3D Visualization FE** for monitoring. **Conflict resolution** is not detailed, but the **Controlling FE** likely prioritizes ACC logic over manual inputs via predefined protocols. **Command execution confirmation** is inferred from the **ACC’s closed-loop operation**, where digital commands are validated against physical responses (e.g., servo position feedback).
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection:
The **Digital Twin’s Technical Connection** leverages a **hybrid communication architecture**:
  - **Intra-system communication** uses **ROS2** (Robot Operating System 2) for message-passing between SW components (e.g., Data Pre-Processing FE ↔ Controlling FE).
  - **Inter-entity communication** relies on **UDP over Wi-Fi** for networked interactions (e.g., Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin). **Network topology** is not explicitly defined, but the architecture suggests a **client-server or peer-to-peer** model with centralized data translation.
  - **Bandwidth requirements** are implied by real-time LiDAR processing (≥10Hz) and high-resolution point cloud data (e.g., 1M+ points/s). **Latency requirements** are critical for control loops (e.g., <100ms for ACC commands).
  - **Reliability mechanisms** include **Data Translation FE** for error-checking and **ROS2’s QoS policies** (e.g., deadlines, reliability settings). **Redundancy** is not mentioned, but UDP’s stateless nature suggests potential for packet loss recovery via retransmission in higher layers.
  - **Security protocols** are not detailed, but **UDP’s transport layer** implies basic protection (e.g., IP filtering), while **ROS2** may use TLS for inter-node encryption if configured. **Data encryption** is not referenced.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **Digital Twin Hosting & Deployment** infrastructure is **edge-centric**, with components distributed across **Physical Twin (vehicle)**, **Digital Twin (onboard/remote)**, and **User Entity (virtual cockpit)**. **Hosting platforms** include:
  - **On-premise/embedded systems** for the **Physical Twin Entity** (e.g., vehicle ECUs running ROS2 nodes).
  - **Edge devices** for **Data Pre-Processing FE** and **Controlling FE** (likely onboard computers with GPU acceleration for LiDAR processing).
  - **Cloud/remote servers** for **Data Storage** and **Reporting FE** (though not explicitly stated, the **Report Generation** implies centralized storage).
**Computational requirements** are high due to:
  - **Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking** (demanding GPU/CPU resources).
  - **Real-time 3D Visualization** (≥10Hz updates for object tracks).
**Storage requirements** are unspecified but likely include:
  - **Raw LiDAR data** (compressed point clouds).
  - **Processed object tracks** (structured trajectories).
  - **Reports** (generated via Reporting FE).
**Scalability mechanisms** are not detailed, but **ROS2’s modularity** allows horizontal scaling of SW components. **Deployment models** include:
  - **Onboard deployment** (for Physical Twin ↔ Digital Twin interaction).
  - **Virtual cockpit deployment** (for User Entity visualization).
**Availability/reliability assurance** is implied by:
  - **ROS2’s fault tolerance** (e.g., node recovery).
  - **UDP’s resilience** (via application-layer retransmission).
  - **Data Translation FE’s validation** (ensuring data integrity).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The lifecycle of the Digital Twin (DT) system, as outlined in the **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard, is structured around an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** with four primary roles: **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**. Each lifecycle phase is governed by these entities, which interact through **Functional Entities (FEs)**—modular components responsible for specific tasks such as data acquisition, transformation, or user interaction.

**Development Stages and Representation Types:**
1. **Conceptualization Phase:** The lifecycle begins with defining the **OME** (e.g., equipment, facilities, or processes) and its observable attributes. The **Digital Twin Entity** is conceptualized as a digital representation of the OME, adhering to the **entity-based RA** framework. This phase involves mapping physical attributes to digital models, often using **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for interoperability, though the standard does not prescribe implementation specifics.
2. **Implementation Phase:** The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** is deployed to interface with sensors/actuators, while the **Digital Twin Entity** is realized as a computational model (e.g., physics-based simulations, rule-based systems, or AI-driven surrogates). The **User Entity** provides interfaces (e.g., dashboards, APIs) for monitoring or control.
3. **Operational Phase:** The DT operates in a **closed-loop** manner, where the **Data Collection Entity** continuously updates the **Digital Twin Entity** with real-time data, while the **User Entity** enables decision-making via analytics or control commands. The **Functional Entities** (e.g., data transformation FEs, visualization FEs) ensure seamless interaction between entities.
4. **Evolution Phase:** The DT evolves through **version control** (implicitly implied by the modular FE architecture) and **transition management**, where updates to the **Digital Twin Entity** (e.g., improved simulation fidelity) or **OME** (e.g., hardware upgrades) are synchronized via the **Data Collection Entity**. The standard does not specify governance tools but emphasizes **interoperability** as a critical enabler.

**Lifecycle Governance:**
Governance is implicitly structured around the **entity-based RA**, where each entity’s responsibilities are clearly delineated. Compliance with **ISO 23247** ensures alignment with manufacturing-specific DT principles, though explicit governance frameworks (e.g., ISO 55000 for asset management) are not referenced. The modularity of FEs allows for **incremental updates** and **plug-and-play** compatibility, facilitating adaptive governance.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** in the **ISO 23247** framework are governed by an **entity-based development methodology**, where each **Functional Entity (FE)** and primary entity (**Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, **User Entity**) follows a **modular, task-specific implementation approach**. The process lacks explicit methodologies but implies the following technical and procedural elements:

**Engineering and Development Processes:**
1. **Entity Decomposition:** The DT is decomposed into **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** (e.g., machinery, processes) and their corresponding **Digital Twin Entities**, which are designed to mirror physical attributes digitally. The **Data Collection Entity** is responsible for **bidirectional data flow** (sensing and actuation), while the **User Entity** provides **human-machine interfaces (HMIs)** or APIs.
2. **Functional Entity (FE) Implementation:** FEs are smaller, specialized components (e.g., **data transformation FEs**, **visualization FEs**, **control FEs**) that can be developed independently. The standard does not mandate technologies but suggests **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** as a potential protocol for interoperability between FEs and entities. For example, a **data transformation FE** might convert raw sensor data (e.g., temperature readings) into a standardized format (e.g., JSON or XML) for the **Digital Twin Entity**.
3. **Integration Framework:** The **entity-based RA** ensures that FEs and entities are integrated via **well-defined interfaces**, enabling **plug-and-play compatibility**. This modularity supports **agile development**, where FEs can be updated or replaced without disrupting the entire DT system.

**Quality Assurance and Testing:**
- **Validation:** The **Digital Twin Entity** must be validated against the **OME** to ensure accuracy in representation. This involves **real-time data reconciliation** between physical and digital states, though specific validation protocols (e.g., statistical tests, error margins) are not detailed in the standard.
- **Testing:** The **Data Collection Entity** undergoes **functional testing** to verify data acquisition and control capabilities. The **User Entity** is tested for **usability** and **responsiveness** in decision-making scenarios. The standard implies **iterative testing** due to the modular FE architecture, allowing for incremental validation.
- **Version Control:** While not explicitly named, the **modular FE design** inherently supports versioning. Updates to individual FEs (e.g., a new **visualization FE**) can be managed via **configuration management tools** (e.g., Git for code-based FEs or enterprise asset management systems for hardware-related FEs).

**Evolution Management Strategies:**
1. **Incremental Updates:** The DT evolves through **modular upgrades**, where FEs or entities are updated independently. For example, replacing a **physics-based simulation FE** with a **machine learning-driven surrogate model FE** would require minimal changes to the broader DT architecture.
2. **Backward Compatibility:** The **entity-based RA** ensures that updates to one entity (e.g., **Digital Twin Entity**) do not break interactions with others (e.g., **Data Collection Entity**), provided standardized interfaces (e.g., OPC UA) are maintained.
3. **Lifecycle Transition Management:** Transitions between lifecycle phases (e.g., from **implementation to operational**) are managed via **handshake protocols** between entities. For instance, the **Data Collection Entity** must confirm successful data synchronization before the **Digital Twin Entity** transitions to a new operational state.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **ISO 23247** framework defines **decision support capabilities** for Digital Twins (DTs) through the **User Entity**, which interacts with the **Digital Twin Entity** to generate insights and facilitate decision-making. The system leverages **entity-based modularity** and **real-time data flows** to enable analytical and operational capabilities, though specific algorithms or confidence measures are not detailed. Key components include:

**Types of Insights Generated:**
1. **Real-Time Monitoring Insights:** The **Digital Twin Entity** processes data from the **Data Collection Entity** to provide **real-time performance metrics** (e.g., equipment health, process efficiency) for the **User Entity**. These insights are derived from **observed deviations** between the **OME** and its digital representation.
2. **Predictive Analytics:** While not explicitly named, the framework implies **predictive capabilities** through the **Digital Twin Entity**, which could integrate **time-series forecasting models** or **anomaly detection algorithms** (e.g., based on historical data from the **Data Collection Entity**).
3. **Optimization Recommendations:** The **User Entity** may generate **control commands** or **optimization suggestions** (e.g., adjusting process parameters) based on insights from the **Digital Twin Entity**. These recommendations are actionable via the **Data Collection Entity**, enabling **closed-loop control**.

**Analytics and Processing Capabilities:**
- **Data Processing:** The **Digital Twin Entity** aggregates and processes data from the **OME** using **Functional Entities (FEs)** specialized in tasks like **data cleaning**, **feature extraction**, or **model updating**. For example, a **data transformation FE** might standardize units or normalize data before feeding it into a **simulation FE**.
- **Model-Driven Analytics:** The **Digital Twin Entity** employs **digital representations** (e.g., physics-based models, rule-based systems) to simulate scenarios or predict outcomes. The **User Entity** can query these models for **what-if analyses** or **root cause diagnostics**.
- **Interoperability:** Analytics are enabled by **standardized data exchange** between entities, with **OPC UA** implied as a protocol for seamless communication. This ensures that insights generated by the **Digital Twin Entity** are accessible to the **User Entity** in a **human-interpretable format**.

**Decision-Making Processes:**
1. **User Interaction Methods:** The **User Entity** provides **interfaces** (e.g., dashboards, APIs, mobile apps) for users to **monitor data**, **execute commands**, or **visualize insights**. These interfaces are designed to support **real-time decision-making** by presenting actionable information (e.g., alerts, recommendations).
2. **Confidence Measures:** While not explicitly quantified, confidence in recommendations is implicitly tied to the **accuracy of the Digital Twin Entity’s representation** of the **OME**. The **Data Collection Entity** ensures data fidelity, and the **User Entity** may validate insights against **ground truth** (e.g., manual inspections) to assess reliability.
3. **Closed-Loop Control:** Decisions made via the **User Entity** (e.g., adjusting a process parameter) are executed through the **Data Collection Entity**, which acts on the **OME**. This **feedback loop** continuously refines the **Digital Twin Entity**, improving future insights.

**Limitations:**
The standard does not specify **advanced analytics** (e.g., deep learning, Bayesian networks) or **confidence thresholds**, leaving these aspects to **implementation-specific choices**. However, the **modular FE architecture** allows for integration of such capabilities in the **Digital Twin Entity** or **User Entity**.
extracted_characteristics.standardization:
The **ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manufacturing** standard provides a **comprehensive compliance framework** for Digital Twin (DT) systems in manufacturing, with a strong emphasis on **interoperability**, **entity-based architecture**, and **industry-specific requirements**. Key aspects include:

**Relevant Industry Standards:**
1. **ISO 23247 (2021):** The foundational standard for DTs in manufacturing, structured into four parts:
   - **Part 2 (Reference Architecture):** Defines the **entity-based RA** with **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity**, and **User Entity**, each composed of **Functional Entities (FEs)**.
   - **Part 3 (Digital Representation):** Specifies requirements for digitally representing physical manufacturing elements (e.g., equipment, processes) with **accuracy and fidelity** constraints.
   - **Part 4 (Information Exchange):** Implies **standardized data formats** and **protocols** for seamless communication between entities.
2. **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA):** While not mandated by ISO 23247, **OPC UA** is referenced as a potential **interoperability standard** for connecting FEs and entities, ensuring **platform-independent data exchange** (e.g., between PLCs, sensors, and DT systems).

**Specifications Followed:**
- **Entity-Based Modularity:** The standard mandates that DTs adhere to the **entity-based RA**, where each entity’s responsibilities are clearly defined. This includes:
  - **Data Collection Entity:** Must implement **bidirectional communication** with the **OME** (e.g., via sensors/actuators).
  - **Digital Twin Entity:** Must provide a **real-time digital representation** of the **OME**, updated via the **Data Collection Entity**.
  - **User Entity:** Must offer **interfaces** (e.g., HMIs, APIs) for **monitoring and control**.
- **Functional Entity (FE) Design:** FEs are required to be **specialized, interchangeable components** (e.g., **data transformation FEs**, **visualization FEs**) that comply with **standardized interfaces** (e.g., OPC UA endpoints).
- **Lifecycle Governance:** The standard implies **compliance with ISO 55000 (Asset Management)** for DT lifecycle management, though explicit alignment is not detailed.

**Certification Requirements:**
- The standard does **not explicitly define certification requirements**, but compliance with **ISO 23247** ensures adherence to **manufacturing-specific DT principles**. Certification may be pursued under broader **industry frameworks** (e.g., **IEC 62443 for cybersecurity** or **IEC 61508 for safety-critical systems**), though these are not referenced in the document.
- **Public Demonstration:** The standard implies **validation through real-world deployment**, as evidenced by the **publicly available DT demonstration** referenced in the document (e.g., [GitHub link](https://github.com/McSCert/Automotive-Digital-Twin)).

**Compliance Frameworks:**
- **Interoperability:** The **entity-based RA** and **FE modularity** ensure **plug-and-play compatibility** between DT components, aligning with **IEC 62890 (Industrial Data Space)** principles.
- **Data Exchange:** While not explicitly named, the standard assumes **standardized data models** (e.g., **ISO 15926 for plant information**) for **Digital Twin Entity** representations.
- **Manufacturing Alignment:** The framework is tailored to **ISO/TC 184/SC 4 (Industrial Automation Systems)** and **ISO/TC 205 (Manufacturing Equipment)** standards, ensuring DTs are **manufacturing-ready**.

**Adopted Interoperability Standards:**
- **OPC UA:** Implied as the **primary interoperability protocol** for connecting FEs and entities, enabling **secure, platform-agnostic communication** (e.g., between **Data Collection Entity** and **Digital Twin Entity**).
- **ISO 15926:** Likely used for **digital representation standards** in the **Digital Twin Entity**, though not explicitly mentioned.
- **IEC 61499:** Could be relevant for **functional block-based implementations** of FEs, though not referenced.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system described in **ISO 26262-1:2018** adheres to **Functional Safety** standards for road vehicles, mandating rigorous cybersecurity and safety protocols. Key measures include:
- **Cybersecurity Protocols**: Compliance with **ISO 26262-8** (if referenced in the broader standard) for hardware and software security, emphasizing secure coding practices, vulnerability management, and protection against cyber-physical attacks.
- **Access Control Mechanisms**: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) and attribute-based access control (ABAC) to restrict system interactions, ensuring only authorized entities (e.g., V2X services, fleet management systems) can modify or access critical twin data.
- **Authentication Systems**: Cryptographic authentication (e.g., **ECC-based digital signatures**) for secure communication between the Digital Twin and external systems, as implied by **3GPP TS 22.185** for V2X services, which requires **end-to-end security** for data integrity and confidentiality.
- **Threat Mitigation Strategies**: Implementation of **ISO 26262-9** (if applicable) for secure software development, including static/dynamic analysis tools (e.g., **Coverity, Fortify**) to detect vulnerabilities in the Digital Twin’s simulation models and real-time data processing pipelines.
- **Fail-Safe Mechanisms**: Redundant fail-safe architectures (e.g., **N-version programming**) to ensure graceful degradation under cyber-attacks or data corruption, aligning with **ISO 26262-5** for system-level safety requirements.
- **Risk Assessment Approaches**: Systematic **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level) decomposition** (per **ISO 26262-3**) to prioritize safety-critical components of the Digital Twin (e.g., collision avoidance simulations) and allocate appropriate security countermeasures.
- **Safety-Critical System Considerations**: Integration of **hardware security modules (HSMs)** for cryptographic key management and **secure boot processes** to prevent tampering with the Digital Twin’s core simulation logic, as implied by **ISO 26262-6** for hardware safety.
- **V2X-Specific Safeguards**: Compliance with **3GPP TS 22.185** for **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) services**, requiring **confidentiality, integrity, and availability (CIA)** for shared Digital Twin data (e.g., traffic simulation updates), with **pre-shared keys (PSKs)** or **certificate-based authentication** for secure communication channels.
- **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) Safety**: As per **ISO 15622**, the Digital Twin must simulate **ACC system failures** (e.g., sensor spoofing, communication delays) and validate fail-safe behaviors (e.g., emergency braking) under **cyber-physical attack scenarios**, ensuring alignment with **ISO 26262-7** for system-level safety validation.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
